title: Today's AI Brief: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including Cognition's $1 billion raise and the challenges of using AI in truth-telling.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
imageCaption: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
imageSourceTitle: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you from the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Cognition, a two-year-old AI coding agent startup, has raised $1 billion at a whopping $26 billion valuation. Their product, Devin, is one of the first AI coding agents on the market. I mean, that's quite a valuation for a two-year-old company - you have to wonder what the investors see in Devin.

According to an interview with Cognition CEO Scott Wu, he's making a bold statement: AI coding agents shouldn't replace humans. This raises questions about the role of AI in software development and the potential for human-AI collaboration. My read is that this is a nuanced view, recognizing that while AI can automate certain tasks, human judgment and creativity are still essential in the development process. It's almost like Scott Wu is saying, 'Hey, AI can help us out, but let's not forget that humans bring something special to the table.'

Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Cognition CEO Scott Wu made headlines again this week when his two-year-old AI coding agent startup raised $1 billion at a $26 billion valuation . Cognition is the maker of Devin, one of the first and, arguably, most successful AI coding agents. Devin, the CEO says, “naturally owns tasks end to end.” In fact, in the blog post announcing that raise, Cognitio... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

We Asked the ‘Future of Truth’ Author to Explain How He Used AI. It Didn’t Go Well is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Earlier this month, WIRED published an excerpt from Steve Rosenbaum’s buzzy new book, The Future of Truth , which looks at how artificial intelligence warps people’s sense of reality. Shortly thereafter, The New York Times reported that the book contained over a half-dozen made-up or misattributed quotes. In a statement, Rosenbaum, who has a master's degree... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
